query: Antigenic Drift occurs in all subtypes of influenza including B

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Differently combined HA and NA antigens are seen in influenza A, which undergo antigenic drifts and shifts resulting in antigenic variation and, thereby, the necessity for vaccine strain types to vary accordingly. Antigenic drifts are genetic changes occurring in the virus due to various actions of polymerases leading to gradual antigenic changes in both HA and NA, producing new variant strains. An antigenic shift occurs when the currently circulating virus disappears and is replaced by a new subtype with novel glycoproteins, to which antibodies against the previously circulating subtype do not cross-react. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are four types of influenza viruses, A, B, C, and D. Influenza types A and B cause human infection annually during the epidemic season. Influenza A has several subtypes according to the combination of hemagglutinin (H) and the neuraminidase (N) proteins that are expressed on the surface of the viruses. There are 18 different hemagglutinin subtypes and 11 different neuraminidase subtypes (H1-18 and N1-11). Influenza A viruses can be characterized by the H and N types such as H1N1 and H3N2 (see Image. Electron Microscopic View of H1N1 Influenza Virus Particles). Influenza B viruses are classified into lineages and strains. Influenza B viruses that have circulated in recent influenza seasons belong to one of two lineages, influenza B Yamagata and influenza B Victoria. Influenza viruses have receptors responsible for making them species-specific.

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza viruses belong to the Orthomyxoviridae RNA virus family and are classified into 3 distinct types based on their major antigenic differences: influenza A, influenza B, and influenza C. Influenza viruses cause annual human epidemics, seasonal and pandemics. Seasonal influenza epidemics caused by influenza A and B viruses result in 3 to 5 million severe cases and thousands of deaths globally each year. The influenza A virus causes epidemics and pandemics because of its spread from migrating birds, pigs, horses, and humans. Transmission can be human to human from fomites, coughing, and sneezing. Influenza B exclusively spreads among humans, underscoring its unique transmission pattern limited to human-to-human contact. This singular mode of transmission excludes involvement with other hosts, distinguishing Influenza B from viruses capable of causing pandemics. Influenza C is a mild disease. This activity describes the mode of action of the influenza vaccine, including modes of administration, formulations, adverse event profiles, eligible patient populations, and monitoring, and it highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the management of influenza patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Oseltamivir -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanism of Resistance: The mechanism of resistance of influenza viruses involves antigenic drift and shift in their surface glycoproteins. Antigenic drift is the process in which minor modifications occur in key viral epitopes through point mutations; antigenic shift results in a complete exchange of hemagglutinin (HA) or neuraminidase (NA) genes. Antigenic shift emerges mainly in influenza A viruses due to their vast animal reservoirs. Antigenic shift is a process frequently associated with the outbreak of pandemics. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The real scientific challenge in the primary strategy for preventing and controlling the influenza virus is antigenic drifts and shifts. Annual vaccination is the current recommendation due to waning immunity. A universal influenza vaccine is undergoing trials and serves the purpose of building a single vaccine that targets all virus strains, which will, in turn, minimize the need for frequent vaccination. [20] [21] Pursuing a universal flu vaccine promises to provide lasting and comprehensive protection against diverse influenza strains, potentially revolutionizing global influenza prevention efforts.

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: H1N1 influenza was first isolated from pigs in the 1930s by researchers in the United States and was subsequently recognized by pork producers and veterinarians as a cause of influenza infections in pigs worldwide. [19] People closely associated with pigs have been known to develop a disease, and pigs have also been infected with human influenza from these handlers. [20] The virus can potentially cause cross-species transmission through viral reassortment. Selection pressure from human populations immune to established strains of influenza can result in changes in antigenic structure over time to evade host immune responses, also known as antigenic drift. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza viruses belong to the Orthomyxoviridae RNA virus family and are classified into 3 distinct types based on their significant antigenic differences: influenza A, influenza B, and influenza C. Influenza viruses cause human epidemics, seasonal and pandemics. Seasonal influenza epidemics caused by influenza A and B viruses result in 3 to 5 million severe cases and thousands of deaths globally yearly.

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Significant shifts in the envelope proteins, known as antigenic shifts, can lead to the emergence of new influenza strains capable of evading the host immune response and facilitating human-to-human transmission in previously susceptible populations. For instance, the 2009 H1N1 pandemic, originating in Mexico, resulted from the combination of multiple strains, including Eurasian avian-like H1N1, avian H1N1, and a previously reassorted lineage consisting of avian H1N1, human H3N2, and swine influenza viruses.

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: For virulence, both neuraminidase and hemagglutinin are vital as they are the key targets of the neutralizing antibodies. Hemagglutinin adheres to the epithelial cells in the respiratory tract allowing for the progression of the infection. Neuraminidase cleaves the bond that holds the virus together and helps to spread the virions. Their H and N proteins identify influenza viruses. An important aspect of the influenza A virus is that it is a genetically labile virus with a high rate of mutations. This results in major changes in antigenic and functional proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Avian Influenza -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Avian influenza, commonly known as "bird flu," is a zoonotic disease caused by avian influenza A viruses. [1] While these viruses primarily infect birds, they can occasionally transmit to humans, resulting in severe illness and potentially death. This disease has garnered significant global attention due to its potential to cause widespread, lethal outbreaks in poultry and severe infections in humans. Highly pathogenic strains are particularly concerning due to their ability to mutate rapidly and spread efficiently, thereby posing serious threats to public health and economic stability. As a result, healthcare providers must maintain high suspicion when identifying and managing cases to prevent rapid disease progression and severe outcomes.Influenza A viruses, part of the Orthomyxoviridae family, [2] [3] are classified into subtypes based on 2 distinct surface glycoproteins—hemagglutinin (H), which has 16 known antigenic variations, and neuraminidase (N), with 9 variations. These subtypes are commonly identified by combinations such as H5N1 or H7N9. [4] The Orthomyxoviridae family also includes influenza B and C viruses, thogotovirus, and isavirus. While influenza B and C viruses can infect certain other species, only influenza A viruses are known to infect birds. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: There are four types of influenza viruses, A, B, C, and D. Influenza types A and B cause human infection annually during the epidemic season. Influenza A has several subtypes according to the combination of hemagglutinin (H) and the neuraminidase (N) proteins that are expressed on the surface of the viruses. There are 18 different hemagglutinin subtypes and 11 different neuraminidase subtypes (H1-18 and N1-11). Influenza A viruses can be characterized by the H and N types such as H1N1 and H3N2. Influenza B viruses are classified into lineages and strains. Influenza B viruses that have circulated in recent influenza seasons belong to one of two lineages, influenza B Yamagata and influenza B Victoria. Influenza viruses have receptors responsible for making them species-specific.

retrieved_docs.title: Avian Influenza -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Avian influenza viruses have a segmented, single-stranded RNA genome, enabling genetic reassortment, antigenic drift, or both, which enhances their ability to evolve and adapt to new hosts. [9] [10] [11] These viruses naturally circulate in wild aquatic birds, such as ducks, geese, and shorebirds, which often carry the virus asymptomatically and act as reservoirs. These birds facilitate the spread of the virus to domestic poultry through direct contact or via contaminated water and environments. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Participants will gain comprehensive insights into the influenza viruses, their classifications (A, B, and C), and the associated human epidemics, seasonal outbreaks, and pandemics. A thorough understanding of influenza viruses, vaccination strategies, and tailored recommendations for diverse patient populations is emphasized. Clinicians will acquire an understanding of the indications for vaccination, incorporating recommendations from the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, targeting specific age groups and individuals with underlying health conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past few decades, more lethal strains of the influenza virus have evolved. Besides infecting humans, these viruses also infect animals. Clinicians should be aware that all rapid tests for detecting influenza have limited sensitivity, and false-negative results are common. The gold standard for diagnosis is the polymerase chain reaction test or viral culture of throat secretions, but these tests take a few days to yield results. The key strategy in reducing the morbidity of the infection is vaccination.

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza strains, including H1N1, are known to transmit between pigs and humans frequently, but human-to-human transmission is uncommon. [27] The 2009 H1N1 strain was easily transmitted between pigs and humans. [28] The potential retention of influenza virus strains in swine after these strains disappear in the human population essentially makes pigs a reservoir where swine influenza viruses can persist. [29] These strains can later emerge to reinfect human populations following reassortment, waned immunity, or a combination of both. As a result, new variants can continue to develop with increased genetic diversity, resulting in new threats to susceptible human populations. [3] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Zanamivir is classified as a member of the neuraminidase inhibitors, a group of direct‑acting antivirals that target the viral neuraminidase enzyme and block influenza virus replication. Because of this mechanism, zanamivir may be used to prevent الإنفلونزا (influenza), highlighting the role of neuraminidase inhibitors in the prophylaxis and management of influenza infections.

Collectively, these entities and relationships define the taxonomy, drug identities, formulation details, lexical variants, contraindications, therapeutic uses, diagnostic screening categories and disease classifications that characterize quinolone antibacterials and related quinoline‑based compounds in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 199

Collectively, these relationships delineate the major antiviral drug classes, their individual members, therapeutic versus preventive applications, structural and mechanistic attributes, and critical contraindications and drug‑interaction considerations essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE constitute a broad therapeutic category, and within this category direct acting antivirals are recognized as a specific subclass. Neuraminidase inhibitors are a particular type of direct acting antiviral, representing a more focused group of agents that act directly to inhibit viral neuraminidase activity. This hierarchical relationship highlights that all neuraminidase inhibitors are direct acting antivirals, and all direct acting antivirals fall under the larger umbrella of systemic-use antivirals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 0 -- Community 6
retrieved_docs.content: these entities demonstrate how diverse chemical classes—DAAs, NRTIs, other nucleoside/nucleotide analogues, integrase inhibitors, NNRTIs, cyclic amines, lipopeptide antivirals, echinocandin antifungals, peptide‑derived radiopharmaceuticals, fusion‑inhibitor peptides, monoclonal antibodies, broad‑spectrum antivirals, and acid‑suppressive agents—are organized by mechanism of action, structural relationships, disease indications, and safety considerations, thereby guiding clinicians in prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of viral infections such as HCV, HIV, cytomegalovirus, orthopoxvirus, and influenza, as well as in related therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 673
retrieved_docs.content: Neuraminidase inhibitors are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, meaning they target viral enzymes essential for replication. Zanamivir is a specific member of the neuraminidase inhibitor class and therefore also functions as a direct‑acting antiviral. By inhibiting the neuraminidase enzyme of the influenza virus, zanamivir may be used to prevent الإنفلونزا (influenza), highlighting its role in both antiviral therapy and prophylaxis against this common respiratory infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: illustrate how antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drugs are organized into mechanistic ATC classes—direct‑acting antivirals, other antivirals, entry inhibitors, integrase inhibitors, NNRTIs, NRTIs, non‑RT nucleoside analogues, neuraminidase inhibitors, and echinocandins—and how specific agents within each class target distinct viral or fungal processes. The relationships also link drugs to preventive or therapeutic indications (HIV prevention, HIV treatment, hepatitis C, hepatitis B/D, influenza, smallpox, viral infection prophylaxis) and to epidemiologic contexts such as HIV infection (a viral infection associated with shared needle use) and influenza‑related pneumonia, thereby supporting informed therapeutic decision‑making and public‑health control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Zanamivir is classified as a member of the neuraminidase inhibitors, which are direct‑acting antivirals. As a neuraminidase inhibitor, zanamivir targets the viral neuraminidase enzyme, thereby blocking the release of progeny influenza viruses and inhibiting viral replication. This relationship places zanamivir within the broader group of direct‑acting antiviral agents that act specifically on viral proteins to treat infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals are a subset of antivirals for systemic use. Within the direct‑acting antiviral category, two major subclasses are identified: cyclic amine direct‑acting antivirals and neuraminidase‑inhibitor direct‑acting antivirals. The drug rimantadine belongs to the cyclic amine direct‑acting antiviral class and is known to produce the physiologic effect of cellular synthetic activity alteration. Zanamivir is a member of the neuraminidase‑inhibitor direct‑acting antiviral class. This hierarchical organization clarifies how specific antiviral agents such as rimantadine and zanamivir fit into broader therapeutic categories and highlights the distinct mechanisms—alteration of cellular synthetic processes for rimantadine and neuraminidase inhibition for zanamivir—by which they exert antiviral activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: preventive and therapeutic use enables precise prescribing, optimized patient care, and effective public‑health interventions across viral diseases such as HIV, ocular viral infections, influenza, smallpox, and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1144
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines that act as direct‑acting antivirals and neuraminidase inhibitors that act as direct‑acting antivirals are both subclasses of the broader category of direct‑acting antivirals. The antiviral drug rimantadine belongs to the cyclic‑amine direct‑acting antiviral class and exerts a physiologic effect characterized by alteration of cellular synthetic activity. Zanamivir belongs to the neuraminidase‑inhibitor direct‑acting antiviral class and can be used to prevent influenza infection. Influenza (الإنفلونزا) is a viral respiratory illness that may be complicated by bacterial pneumonia caused by cocci (التهاب رئوي بالعقديات). The antibiotic gemifloxacin is an appropriate treatment for this type of bacterial pneumonia. Thus, the knowledge links specific antiviral subclasses to their representative drugs, describes the mechanism of rimantadine, highlights zanamivir’s preventive role against influenza, and connects influenza to its potential bacterial pneumonia complication and its treatment with gemifloxacin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: include the fluoroquinolone gemifloxacin for cocci‑related pneumonia, and the monoclonal antibody abciximab for thrombotic conditions. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are antiviral agents indicated for smallpox, and zanamivir is used prophylactically against influenza, a viral respiratory illness that can progress to pneumonia with nodular infiltrates. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured taxonomy of antiviral, antibacterial, antifungal, anticoagulant, radiopharmaceutical and immunomodulatory agents organized by chemical class, mechanism of action, and clinical indication, thereby supporting precise prescribing, safety monitoring, and diagnostic use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 0 -- Community 20
retrieved_docs.content: therapies. Clinically, gemifloxacin may treat granulomatous pneumonia, a pulmonary complication that can follow influenza infection; zanamivir provides prophylaxis against influenza. Smallpox, an orthopoxvirus disease, can be treated with brincidofovir or tecovirimat. Together, these classifications, molecular targets, mechanisms of action, contraindications, and formulation details constitute an integrated framework for antiviral therapy, anticoagulation management, antifungal treatment, and peptide‑based diagnostic imaging in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations are classified as direct acting antivirals. Within this subclass, the combination regimens abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine are listed as members. The single agent abacavir is chemically related to dideoxyadenosin, and both combination products are mapped to the dideoxyadenosin scaffold because they contain abacavir. Cyclic amines that act as direct acting antivirals constitute another subclass; rimantadin belongs to this cyclic‑amine subclass and produces the physiologic effect known as Cellular Synthetic Activity Alteration. Neuraminidase inhibitors are also a subclass of direct acting antivirals, and zanamivir is a member of this subclass; zanamivir can prevent influenza (الإنفلونزا). Collectively, these connections show how antiviral agents are organized by mechanism (direct acting), chemical class (cyclic amines, neuraminidase inhibitors), and therapeutic use (HIV combination therapy, influenza prophylaxis), and how individual drugs such as rimantadin and zanamivir exert distinct biological effects.

Zanamivir is identified as a prophylactic agent for الإنفلونزا, consistent with its role as an antiviral used for influenza prevention. Collectively, these classifications, combination formulations, preventive and therapeutic agents, interaction contraindications, behavioral risk factors, immunological adjuvant concepts, and disease‑taxonomy considerations provide an integrated framework for the clinical management of HIV, influenza, coccidioidal pneumonia, and related viral and fungal infections.
